question_text: A student observes that during boiling, bubbles form inside a liquid and rise to the surface, whereas during evaporation, particles leave only from the surface. Which statement correctly compares the particle arrangement and energy changes in these two processes?

options.label: A
options.text: Boiling occurs throughout the liquid because heat energy allows particles everywhere to overcome intermolecular forces, while evaporation is slower because only surface particles have enough energy

options.label: B
options.text: Boiling is faster because the liquid is hotter, but both processes require the same total latent heat per unit mass

options.label: C
options.text: Evaporation happens only at the surface because air pressure prevents boiling from occurring inside the liquid

options.label: D
options.text: Boiling and evaporation are the same process; the difference is only in how quickly they happen

correct_answer: A) Boiling occurs throughout the liquid because heat energy allows particles everywhere to overcome intermolecular forces, while evaporation is slower because only surface particles have enough energy
explanation: Boiling is a rapid, violent process where particles throughout the liquid gain enough kinetic energy to break free and form gas bubbles everywhere. Evaporation is slower because only surface particles with above-average energy can escape, so fewer particles leave per unit time.
